entries.name: Sydney Opera House
entries.desc: Jørn Utzon's harborside performing-arts center, its shell-like concrete roofs an icon of 20th-century Expressionist Modern architecture.

photo.alt: Sydney Opera House's white shell roofs on Sydney Harbour
photo.credit: <!-- photo: sydney-opera-house.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Exterior of Sydney Opera House.jpg" by BennyG3255 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Exterior_of_Sydney_Opera_House.jpg -->

entries.name: Schönbrunn Palace
entries.desc: The Baroque summer residence of the Habsburgs, with hundreds of Rococo rooms and extensive formal gardens on the edge of Vienna.

photo.alt: Schönbrunn Palace facade and gardens in Vienna
photo.credit: <!-- photo: schonbrunn-palace.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC0 · "Schönbrunn Palace, Vienna.JPG" by Gveret Tered · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Sch%C3%B6nbrunn_Palace,_Vienna.JPG -->

entries.name: Heydar Aliyev Center
entries.desc: Zaha Hadid's cultural center in Baku, a flowing white shell whose walls, roof, and plaza merge into one continuous curving surface without sharp angles.

photo.alt: Heydar Aliyev Center's flowing white curved facade in Baku
photo.credit: <!-- photo: heydar-aliyev-center.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Heydar Aliyev Center 1.1.jpg" by Emin Allahverdi · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Heydar_Aliyev_Center_1.1.jpg -->

entries.name: Cathedral of Brasília
entries.desc: Oscar Niemeyer's Roman Catholic cathedral formed of sweeping hyperboloid concrete ribs, with a glass roof filling the sunken nave with light.

photo.alt: Cathedral of Brasília's hyperboloid concrete crown
photo.credit: <!-- photo: cathedral-of-brasilia.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Catedral Metropolitana de Brasilia.jpg" by Tissiana de A. de Souza · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Catedral_Metropolitana_de_Brasilia.jpg -->

entries.name: National Congress of Brazil
entries.desc: Oscar Niemeyer's seat of Brazil's legislature, composed of twin towers flanked by a dome and an upturned bowl housing the two chambers.

photo.alt: National Congress of Brazil's twin towers, dome, and bowl in Brasília
photo.credit: <!-- photo: national-congress-brazil.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.5 · "Brazilian National Congress.jpg" by Eurico Zimbres · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Brazilian_National_Congress.jpg -->

entries.name: Angkor Wat
entries.desc: A vast moated Khmer temple complex, originally Hindu and later Buddhist, celebrated for its towering lotus-bud spires and bas-relief galleries.

photo.alt: Angkor Wat's lotus-bud towers rising above its moat
photo.credit: <!-- photo: angkor-wat.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Angkor Wat.jpg" by Bjørn Christian Tørrissen · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Angkor_Wat.jpg -->

entries.name: Habitat 67
entries.desc: Moshe Safdie's housing complex built for Expo 67, a stacked arrangement of prefabricated concrete modules that give each apartment a private terrace, exploring whether mass housing could feel like a house.

photo.alt: Habitat 67's stacked concrete modules in Montreal
photo.credit: <!-- photo: habitat-67.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Habitat 67 Montreal, QC.jpg" by Shawn M. Kent · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Habitat_67_Montreal,_QC.jpg -->

entries.name: Great Wall of China
entries.desc: A series of fortifications built over many centuries, stretching thousands of miles across northern China; the most visited sections at Badaling and Mutianyu near Beijing date to the Ming Dynasty.

photo.alt: The Great Wall of China winding across mountain ridgelines
photo.credit: <!-- photo: great-wall-of-china.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC0 · "Great Wall of China July 2006.JPG" by Velatrix · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Great_Wall_of_China_July_2006.JPG -->

entries.name: Forbidden City
entries.desc: The Ming and Qing dynasty imperial palace complex at the center of Beijing, a walled city of 980 buildings in traditional Chinese palatial architecture — the largest collection of preserved ancient wooden structures in the world.

photo.alt: Aerial view over the walled Forbidden City complex in Beijing
photo.credit: <!-- photo: forbidden-city-beijing.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "China (Beijing) Aerial view of Forbidden City" by Güldem Üstün · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:China_(Beijing)_Aerial_view_of_Forbidden_City_(38884882275).jpg -->

entries.name: Beijing National Stadium (Bird's Nest)
entries.desc: The main stadium of the 2008 Summer Olympics, wrapped in a lattice of interlacing steel that gives it its bird's-nest nickname.

photo.alt: The lattice steel exterior of Beijing National Stadium under clear sky
photo.credit: <!-- photo: beijing-national-stadium-birds-nest.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Beijing National Stadium from the Central Axis" by N509FZ · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Beijing_National_Stadium_from_the_Central_Axis_(20220905140702).jpg -->

entries.name: CCTV Headquarters
entries.desc: A headquarters for Chinese state television by OMA, formed of two leaning towers joined at top and bottom into a continuous looped ring.

photo.alt: The two leaning towers of CCTV Headquarters joined into a continuous loop
photo.credit: <!-- photo: cctv-headquarters-beijing.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Guanghua Road Office Area of CMG" by HoweyYuan · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Guanghua_Road_Office_Area_of_CMG_(20240907141412).jpg -->

entries.name: Shanghai Tower
entries.desc: A twisting supertall tower in Shanghai's Lujiazui district by Gensler, wrapped in a double-skin glass facade that spirals as it rises.

photo.alt: The twisting glass facade of Shanghai Tower rising into the sky
photo.credit: <!-- photo: shanghai-tower.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC0 · "Shanghai Shanghai Tower 5166304" by Ermell · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Shanghai_Shanghai_Tower_5166304.jpg -->

entries.name: Potala Palace
entries.desc: A monumental hilltop palace in Lhasa, former winter residence of the Dalai Lamas, rising in tiers of white and red walls above the city.

photo.alt: The tiered white and red walls of Potala Palace rising above Lhasa
photo.credit: <!-- photo: potala-palace-lhasa.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "Tibet-5483 - Potala Palace" by Dennis G. Jarvis · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Tibet-5483_-_Potala_Palace.jpg -->

entries.name: Villa Tugendhat
entries.desc: Mies van der Rohe's residence in Brno, its open plan defined by a curved ebony wood wall and a straight onyx wall under a flat roof, with full-height glazing that slides into the floor to open the living space to the garden — a masterpiece of modern residential architecture.

photo.alt: The glazed garden facade of Villa Tugendhat by Mies van der Rohe
photo.credit: <!-- photo: villa-tugendhat-brno.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Vila Tugendhat exterior Dvorak2" by Petr1987 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Vila_Tugendhat_exterior_Dvorak2.JPG -->

entries.name: Great Pyramid of Giza
entries.desc: Tomb of the pharaoh Khufu and the only surviving wonder of the ancient world, built of millions of limestone blocks on the Giza plateau.

photo.alt: The Great Pyramid of Giza rising from the desert plateau
photo.credit: <!-- photo: great-pyramid-of-giza.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Great Pyramid of Giza" by kallerna · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Great_Pyramid_of_Giza.jpg -->

entries.name: Temple of Karnak
entries.tag: Luxor, Egypt · Ancient Egyptian Temple Complex
entries.desc: A sprawling temple precinct built over many centuries, best known for its great hypostyle hall crowded with massive carved columns.

photo.alt: Massive carved papyrus columns in the Great Hypostyle Hall at Karnak
photo.credit: <!-- photo: karnak-temple-hypostyle-hall.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Pharonic Columns in Karnak Temple , Luxor" by Belmahdy · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Pharonic_Columns_in_Karnak_Temple_,_Luxor.JPG -->

entries.name: Abu Simbel
entries.tag: Aswan Governorate, Egypt · Ancient Egyptian Rock-Cut Temple
entries.desc: Two temples carved into a cliff face on the west bank of the Nile by Ramesses II, fronted by four colossal seated statues; the entire complex was relocated in the 1960s to save it from the rising waters of Lake Nasser.

photo.alt: Colossal seated statues of Ramesses II at the rock-cut facade of the Great Temple of Abu Simbel
photo.credit: <!-- photo: abu-simbel-statues.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "StatuesofRamesesIIAbu SimbelEgypt.jpg" by Brian Snelson · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:StatuesofRamesesIIAbu_SimbelEgypt.jpg -->

entries.name: Pont du Gard
entries.desc: A three-tiered Roman aqueduct bridge crossing the Gardon river, the highest surviving Roman aqueduct and a landmark of Roman engineering built without mortar.

photo.alt: The three-tiered Roman aqueduct bridge of Pont du Gard crossing the Gardon river
photo.credit: <!-- photo: pont-du-gard.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Pont du Gard, Nîmes 17.jpg" by John Samuel · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Pont_du_Gard,_N%C3%AEmes_17.jpg -->

entries.name: Notre-Dame de Paris
entries.desc: A landmark of French Gothic architecture on the Île de la Cité, noted for its rib vaults, flying buttresses, and rose windows; damaged by fire in 2019 and restored.

photo.alt: West facade of Notre-Dame de Paris with its portals, tympanums, and gallery of kings
photo.credit: <!-- photo: notre-dame-paris-facade.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Façade et portails de Notre Dame de Paris.JPG" by Loïc LLH · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Fa%C3%A7ade_et_portails_de_Notre_Dame_de_Paris.JPG -->

entries.name: Chartres Cathedral
entries.desc: One of the best-preserved High Gothic cathedrals, famous for its extensive medieval stained glass and mismatched pair of spires.

photo.alt: Chartres Cathedral with its mismatched pair of spires
photo.credit: <!-- photo: chartres-cathedral-west-facade.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Cathédrale Notre-Dame (Chartres) (2).jpg" by Gzen92 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Cath%C3%A9drale_Notre-Dame_(Chartres)_(2).jpg -->

entries.name: Sainte-Chapelle
entries.desc: A royal chapel built to house relics of the Passion, its upper level an almost continuous wall of towering stained-glass windows.

photo.alt: The upper chapel of Sainte-Chapelle with its towering stained-glass windows
photo.credit: <!-- photo: sainte-chapelle-stained-glass.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Sainte Chapelle Interior Stained Glass.jpg" by Oldmanisold · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Sainte_Chapelle_Interior_Stained_Glass.jpg -->

entries.name: Reims Cathedral
entries.desc: The traditional coronation church of French kings, a High Gothic cathedral known for its sculpted west front and the smiling angel statue.

photo.alt: The sculpted west facade of Reims Cathedral, the coronation church of French kings
photo.credit: <!-- photo: reims-cathedral-facade.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Reims Cathedral Facade 1.jpg" by Ad Meskens · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Reims_Cathedral_Facade_1.jpg -->

entries.name: Mont Saint-Michel
entries.desc: A tidal island off the Normandy coast crowned by a Gothic abbey, its spired silhouette rising above a cluster of medieval streets and ramparts, surrounded at high tide by the sea.

photo.alt: Mont Saint-Michel tidal island with its Gothic abbey rising above the ramparts
photo.credit: <!-- photo: mont-saint-michel.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Mont St Michel in the afternoon.jpg" by Lynx1211 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Mont_St_Michel_in_the_afternoon.jpg -->

entries.name: Château de Chambord
entries.desc: A Loire Valley royal hunting lodge that grew into the largest château in the region, with a roofscape of lanterns, dormers, and chimneys; its double-helix staircase is widely attributed to Leonardo da Vinci.

photo.alt: Aerial view of Château de Chambord showing its roofscape of lanterns, dormers, and chimneys
photo.credit: <!-- photo: chateau-de-chambord-aerial.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Aerial image of Château de Chambord (view from the southeast).jpg" by Carsten Steger · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Aerial_image_of_Ch%C3%A2teau_de_Chambord_(view_from_the_southeast).jpg -->

meta: Largest Château in the Loire Valley

entries.name: Palace of Versailles
entries.desc: The lavish seat of the French monarchy under Louis XIV, known for its Hall of Mirrors, state apartments, and vast formal gardens.

photo.alt: Palace of Versailles facade viewed across the marble courtyard
photo.credit: <!-- photo: palace-of-versailles.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Façade of The Palace of Versailles" by Jorge Láscar · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Fa%C3%A7ade_of_The_Palace_of_Versailles_(24194247422).jpg -->

entries.name: Villa Savoye
entries.desc: Le Corbusier's white villa raised on slender pilotis, a clear demonstration of his five points of modern architecture and the International Style.

photo.alt: Villa Savoye's white pilotis-raised exterior, Poissy
photo.credit: <!-- photo: villa-savoye.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Villa Savoye, Poissy éléments de décoration circulaires" by Cbyd · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Villa_Savoye,_Poissy_%C3%A9l%C3%A9ments_de_d%C3%A9coration_circulaires.jpg -->

entries.name: Unité d'Habitation
entries.desc: Le Corbusier's residential block in Marseille raised on massive concrete pilotis, with duplex apartments, an internal shopping street, and communal roof terrace — a blueprint for social housing that shaped urban architecture worldwide for decades.

photo.alt: Unité d'Habitation raised on massive concrete pilotis, Marseille
photo.credit: <!-- photo: cite-radieuse-marseille.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC0 · "Cité Radieuse à Marseille" by Gatotienesueño · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Cit%C3%A9_Radieuse_%C3%A0_Marseille.jpg -->

entries.name: Chapel of Notre-Dame du Haut (Ronchamp)
entries.desc: A pilgrimage chapel by Le Corbusier in the Haute-Saône hills, its sculptural white shell roof, asymmetric walls, and deeply splayed colored-glass windows a departure from everything he had built before.

photo.alt: Bell wall with the chapel's curved white roof shell behind, Ronchamp
photo.credit: <!-- photo: notre-dame-du-haut-ronchamp.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Campanile (Ronchamp)" by Gzen92 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Campanile_(Ronchamp).jpg -->

entries.name: Centre Pompidou
entries.desc: A pioneering High-Tech arts complex by Piano and Rogers that turns the building inside out, exposing its structure, ducts, and escalators on the exterior.

photo.alt: Centre Pompidou's exposed structural frame and colored ducts, illuminated at night
photo.credit: <!-- photo: centre-pompidou.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Pompidou Centre" by Infinitescreen · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Pompidou_Centre.jpg -->

entries.name: Fondation Louis Vuitton
entries.desc: Frank Gehry's art foundation in the Bois de Boulogne, wrapped in billowing curved glass sails over a structure of white concrete blocks.

photo.alt: Fondation Louis Vuitton's glass sails rising above the Bois de Boulogne
photo.credit: <!-- photo: fondation-louis-vuitton.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 3.0 · "Fondation Louis Vuitton 5" by Moktarama · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Fondation_Louis_Vuitton_5.jpg -->

entries.name: Cologne Cathedral
entries.desc: A twin-spired Gothic cathedral begun in the 13th century and completed in the 19th, long among the tallest churches in the world.

photo.alt: Cologne Cathedral's twin Gothic spires
photo.credit: <!-- photo: cologne-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Cologne Germany Exterior-view-of-Cologne-Cathedral-01" by CEphoto, Uwe Aranas · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Cologne_Germany_Exterior-view-of-Cologne-Cathedral-01.jpg -->

entries.name: Bauhaus Building
entries.desc: Walter Gropius's school building for the Bauhaus in Dessau, its glass curtain-wall workshop wing and asymmetric interlocking volumes a direct expression of the school's design philosophy and one of the most influential buildings of the 20th century.

photo.alt: Bauhaus Dessau's glass curtain-wall workshop wing
photo.credit: <!-- photo: bauhaus-dessau.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "6268 Dessau" by Spyrosdrakopoulos · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:6268_Dessau.JPG -->

entries.name: Neue Nationalgalerie
entries.desc: Mies van der Rohe's final major building, a glass-walled steel pavilion raised on a granite platform in Berlin; its vast flat roof is supported by only eight cruciform columns at its perimeter, leaving the interior almost entirely column-free.

photo.alt: Neue Nationalgalerie glass pavilion, Berlin
photo.credit: <!-- photo: neue-nationalgalerie-berlin.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Berlin Neue Nationalgalerie dk0876.jpg" by DrKssn · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Berlin_Neue_Nationalgalerie_dk0876.jpg -->

entries.name: Olympic Stadium Munich
entries.desc: The main venue of the 1972 Summer Olympics, its sweeping tent-like roof of translucent acrylic panels suspended from steel masts by cables, a landmark of structural engineering and a precedent for all large tensile roofscapes that followed.

photo.alt: Munich Olympic Stadium tensile roof, viewed from Olympic Tower
photo.credit: <!-- photo: olympic-stadium-munich.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "2014 Olympiastadion Munich.jpg" by M(e)ister Eiskalt · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:2014_Olympiastadion_Munich.jpg -->

entries.name: Jewish Museum Berlin
entries.desc: Daniel Libeskind's zinc-clad extension for Berlin's Jewish Museum, a fractured zigzag cut with voids and skewed windows, its disorienting interiors designed to evoke the history of German Jews.

photo.alt: Jewish Museum Berlin zinc-clad exterior
photo.credit: <!-- photo: jewish-museum-berlin.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Libeskind-Bau (Jüdisches Museum Berlin).jpg" by Avi1111 dr. avishai teicher · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Libeskind-Bau_(J%C3%BCdisches_Museum_Berlin).jpg -->

entries.name: Parthenon
entries.desc: Marble temple to Athena crowning the Acropolis, the defining monument of Classical Greek Doric architecture and a model of proportion imitated for centuries.

photo.alt: Parthenon temple on the Acropolis, Athens
photo.credit: <!-- photo: parthenon-athens.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 4.0 · "Parthenon on Acropolis in Athens.jpg" by acediscovery · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Parthenon_on_Acropolis_in_Athens.jpg -->

entries.name: Harpa Concert Hall
entries.desc: Reykjavik's concert hall and conference center on the harbor, its facade a grid of modular geometric glass panels designed with artist Olafur Eliasson that shift color with changing light and the Northern sky.

photo.alt: Harpa Concert Hall exterior in snow, Reykjavik harbor
photo.credit: <!-- photo: harpa-concert-hall-reykjavik.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Snow in Reykjavik, Harpa concert hall (6905136669).jpg" by Tristan Ferne · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Snow_in_Reykjavik,_Harpa_concert_hall_(6905136669).jpg -->

entries.name: Taj Mahal
entries.desc: A white marble mausoleum commissioned by Emperor Shah Jahan for his wife, the crowning example of Mughal architecture with its symmetrical domed form and gardens.

photo.alt: Taj Mahal mausoleum, Agra
photo.credit: <!-- photo: taj-mahal-agra.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Taj Mahal, Agra, India.jpg" by Yann · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Taj_Mahal,_Agra,_India.jpg -->

entries.name: Capitol Complex
entries.desc: Le Corbusier's government precinct for the planned city of Chandigarh, a group of monumental raw-concrete buildings around a civic plaza.

photo.alt: Palace of Assembly, Chandigarh Capitol Complex
photo.credit: <!-- photo: capitol-complex-chandigarh.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "The Palace of Assembly.jpg" by Lillottama · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:The_Palace_of_Assembly.jpg -->

entries.name: Lotus Temple
entries.desc: A Bahá'í house of worship in New Delhi shaped like an opening lotus flower, formed of clustered white marble petals open to visitors of all faiths.

photo.alt: Lotus Temple, New Delhi
photo.credit: <!-- photo: lotus-temple-new-delhi.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Lotus Temple, located in Delhi, India, is a Bahá'í House of Worship.jpg" by Buete · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Lotus_Temple,_located_in_Delhi,_India,_is_a_Bah%C3%A1%27%C3%AD_House_of_Worship.jpg -->

entries.name: Borobudur
entries.desc: The world's largest Buddhist temple, a nine-tiered stone monument on the island of Java covered in 2,672 relief panels and 504 Buddha statues, built under the Sailendra dynasty.

photo.alt: Borobudur temple viewed from above, Java
photo.credit: <!-- photo: borobudur-temple.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Borobudur temple from above.jpg" by Thebhe86 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Borobudur_temple_from_above.jpg -->

entries.name: Colosseum
entries.desc: The largest amphitheater of the Roman world, built of stone and concrete with tiered arcades, once seating tens of thousands for gladiatorial games.

photo.alt: The Colosseum amphitheater, Rome
photo.credit: <!-- photo: colosseum-rome.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Colosseum in rome.jpg" by Silviomerci1971 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Colosseum_in_rome.jpg -->

entries.name: Pantheon
entries.desc: Roman temple, later a church, famous for its unreinforced concrete dome and central oculus, long the largest such dome ever built.

photo.alt: The Pantheon's facade and portico, Rome
photo.credit: <!-- photo: pantheon-rome.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.5 · "Rome Pantheon front.jpg" by Roberta Dragan · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Rome_Pantheon_front.jpg -->

entries.name: Doge's Palace
entries.desc: The seat of Venetian government for centuries, its distinctive facade of pink Verona marble over delicate loggia arcades is a defining example of Venetian Gothic architecture on the Piazzetta.

photo.alt: Doge's Palace facade facing the water, Venice
photo.credit: <!-- photo: doges-palace-venice.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "(Venice) Doge's Palace facing the sea.jpg" by Didier Descouens · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:(Venice)_Doge%27s_Palace_facing_the_sea.jpg -->

entries.name: Duomo di Milano
entries.desc: A vast marble cathedral built over nearly six centuries, bristling with spires, pinnacles, and statuary in an elaborate late Gothic style.

photo.alt: Milan Cathedral's Gothic facade and spires
photo.credit: <!-- photo: duomo-di-milano.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Milan Cathedral (Duomo di Milano).jpg" by NahidSultan · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Milan_Cathedral_(Duomo_di_Milano).jpg -->

entries.name: Florence Cathedral (Brunelleschi's Dome)
entries.desc: Brunelleschi's octagonal brick dome crowns Florence's cathedral, an engineering breakthrough built without full scaffolding that helped launch Renaissance architecture.

photo.alt: Florence Cathedral with Brunelleschi's dome
photo.credit: <!-- photo: florence-cathedral-duomo.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Florence Cathedral (Duomo).jpg" by Kevin Poh · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Florence_Cathedral_(Duomo).jpg -->

entries.name: Villa Rotonda
entries.desc: Andrea Palladio's symmetrical country villa with a central dome and four temple-front porticoes, a hugely influential model for later Palladian architecture.

photo.alt: Villa Rotonda's symmetrical facade, Vicenza
photo.credit: <!-- photo: villa-rotonda-vicenza.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Vicenza Villa Rotonda La Capra 01.jpg" by Zairon · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Vicenza_Villa_Rotonda_La_Capra_01.jpg -->

entries.name: Bosco Verticale
entries.desc: A pair of Milan residential towers by Stefano Boeri whose balconies host thousands of trees and shrubs, an influential example of vertical greening.

photo.alt: Bosco Verticale's tree-covered residential towers, Milan
photo.credit: <!-- photo: bosco-verticale-milan.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 3.0 · "Il Bosco Verticale Milano Px (137734459).jpeg" by Jeronimo Alcala · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Il_Bosco_Verticale_Milano_Px_(137734459).jpeg -->

entries.name: Hōryū-ji
entries.desc: A Buddhist temple complex near Nara whose timber pagoda and hall are counted among the oldest surviving wooden buildings in the world.

photo.alt: Five-storied wooden pagoda at Hōryū-ji temple complex in Ikaruga, Nara
photo.credit: <!-- photo: horyuji-five-storied-pagoda.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC0 · "Five-storied Pagoda - Hōryū-ji - Ikaruga, Nara, Japan - DSC07559.jpg" by Daderot · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Five-storied_Pagoda_-_H%C5%8Dry%C5%AB-ji_-_Ikaruga,_Nara,_Japan_-_DSC07559.jpg -->

entries.name: Himeji Castle
entries.desc: A well-preserved hilltop fortress of white plastered walls and layered roofs, considered the finest surviving example of Japanese feudal castle architecture.

photo.alt: White plastered walls and layered roofs of Himeji Castle on its hilltop
photo.credit: <!-- photo: himeji-castle.jpg — Wikimedia Commons Public Domain · "Himeji Castle 0804 1.jpg" by Gorgo · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Himeji_Castle_0804_1.jpg -->

entries.name: Nakagin Capsule Tower
entries.desc: Kisho Kurokawa's tower of prefabricated living capsules bolted to concrete cores, the best-known built work of Japan's Metabolist movement; demolished in 2022.

photo.alt: Nakagin Capsule Tower's stacked prefabricated capsules in Tokyo
photo.credit: <!-- photo: nakagin-capsule-tower.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Nakagin Capsule Tower 03.jpg" by Chris 73 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Nakagin_Capsule_Tower_03.jpg -->

entries.name: Al-Khazneh (The Treasury)
entries.desc: An ornate facade carved directly into rose-red sandstone by the Nabataeans, the most famous monument of the ancient caravan city of Petra.

photo.alt: Al-Khazneh facade carved into rose-red sandstone at Petra
photo.credit: <!-- photo: al-khazneh-petra.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Al-Khazneh, Petra.jpg" by Imam Khairul Annas · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Al-Khazneh,_Petra.jpg -->

entries.name: Petronas Towers
entries.desc: Twin steel-and-glass towers by César Pelli linked by a double-decker skybridge, once the tallest buildings in the world, with floor plans drawn from Islamic geometry.

photo.alt: Petronas Twin Towers skyline in Kuala Lumpur with the skybridge connecting them
photo.credit: <!-- photo: petronas-towers.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Petronas Towers (18980016671).jpg" by Jorge Láscar · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Petronas_Towers_(18980016671).jpg -->

entries.name: El Castillo (Chichen Itza)
entries.desc: A step pyramid dedicated to the feathered serpent deity Kukulkán, the centerpiece of the Maya city of Chichen Itza; designed so the equinox sun casts a serpent shadow down its staircase.

photo.alt: El Castillo step pyramid at the center of Chichen Itza
photo.credit: <!-- photo: el-castillo-chichen-itza.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Chichen Itza 3.jpg" by Daniel Schwen · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Chichen_Itza_3.jpg -->

entries.name: Oslo Opera House
entries.desc: The Norwegian National Opera's home on the Oslo waterfront by Snøhetta, its white marble and granite roof slopes to the fjord so visitors can walk directly onto the building's angled exterior.

photo.alt: Oslo Opera House's white marble roof slopes down to the waterfront
photo.credit: <!-- photo: oslo-opera-house.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Oslo Opera House seen from Langkaia.JPG" by Helge Høifødt · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Oslo_Opera_House_seen_from_Langkaia.JPG -->

entries.name: Machu Picchu
entries.desc: A 15th-century Inca citadel set high on an Andean ridge, renowned for its precise dry-stone masonry and dramatic terraced setting.

photo.alt: Machu Picchu's terraced Inca citadel set on an Andean ridge
photo.credit: <!-- photo: machu-picchu.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "Machu Picchu, Peru.jpg" by Pedro Szekely · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Machu_Picchu,_Peru.jpg -->

entries.name: St. Basil's Cathedral
entries.desc: An Orthodox church on Red Square commissioned by Ivan the Terrible, instantly recognizable for its cluster of colorful, patterned onion domes.

photo.alt: St. Basil's Cathedral on Red Square, Moscow
photo.credit: <!-- photo: st-basils-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 4.0 · "Saint Basil's Cathedral, Red Square, Moscow, Russia.jpg" by Vyacheslav Argenberg · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Saint_Basil%27s_Cathedral,_Red_Square,_Moscow,_Russia.jpg -->

entries.name: Winter Palace
entries.desc: The former principal residence of Russian emperors, an Elizabethan Baroque palace on the Neva that now forms part of the Hermitage Museum.

photo.alt: The Winter Palace facade on the Neva, St. Petersburg
photo.credit: <!-- photo: winter-palace-st-petersburg.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "3018. St. Petersburg. Winter Palace.jpg" by GAlexandrova · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:3018._St._Petersburg._Winter_Palace.jpg -->

entries.name: Marina Bay Sands
entries.desc: Moshe Safdie's resort of three hotel towers topped by a boat-shaped SkyPark, a defining feature of Singapore's Marina Bay waterfront.

photo.alt: Marina Bay Sands resort towers and SkyPark, Singapore
photo.credit: <!-- photo: marina-bay-sands.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Marina Bay Sands, Singapore 1.jpg" by Ray in Manila · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Marina_Bay_Sands,_Singapore_1.jpg -->

entries.name: Gardens by the Bay (Supertree Grove)
entries.desc: A Singapore nature park whose Supertree Grove features tall tree-shaped vertical gardens linked by an elevated walkway, alongside climate-controlled conservatories.

photo.alt: Supertree Grove towers at Gardens by the Bay, Singapore
photo.credit: <!-- photo: gardens-by-the-bay-supertree-grove.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Singapore Gardens by the Bay Supertree Grove 22.jpg" by Zairon · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Singapore_Gardens_by_the_Bay_Supertree_Grove_22.jpg -->

entries.name: Jewel Changi Airport
entries.desc: A retail and garden complex at Changi Airport by Moshe Safdie, centered on an indoor waterfall that pours through the middle of a glass-domed roof.

photo.alt: Rain Vortex waterfall beneath the glass dome, Jewel Changi Airport
photo.credit: <!-- photo: jewel-changi-airport-rain-vortex.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "JewelSingaporeVortex1.jpg" by Matteo Morando · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:JewelSingaporeVortex1.jpg -->

entries.name: Burgos Cathedral
entries.desc: A Spanish Gothic cathedral built over several centuries, distinguished by its openwork spires, star-vaulted crossing, and richly carved chapels.

photo.alt: Burgos Cathedral facade and spires, Spain
photo.credit: <!-- photo: burgos-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons Public Domain · "Burgos Cathedral 01.jpg" by Daniel Canton (assembled by Xavax) · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Burgos_Cathedral_01.jpg -->

entries.name: Alhambra
entries.desc: A hilltop palace and fortress of the Nasrid dynasty, celebrated for its intricate stucco, muqarnas ceilings, tilework, and courtyard gardens.

photo.alt: The Alhambra palace and fortress complex, Granada
photo.credit: <!-- photo: alhambra-granada.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC0 · "The whole Alhambra Granada Spain.jpg" by Jebulon · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:The_whole_Alhambra_Granada_Spain.jpg -->

entries.name: Sagrada Família
entries.desc: Antoni Gaudí's still-unfinished basilica, an extraordinary fusion of Gothic and organic Art Nouveau forms with soaring towers and tree-like columns.

photo.alt: Sagrada Família basilica towers, Barcelona
photo.credit: <!-- photo: sagrada-familia.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Sagrada Familia 01.jpg" by Bernard Gagnon · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Sagrada_Familia_01.jpg -->

entries.name: Casa Batlló
entries.desc: A Gaudí-remodeled townhouse on Passeig de Gràcia, known for its undulating facade, bone-like balconies, and shimmering broken-tile mosaic surfaces.

photo.alt: Upper facade and rooftop of Casa Batlló, Barcelona
photo.credit: <!-- photo: casa-batllo-facade.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Casa Batlló 01.jpg" by Bernard Gagnon · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Casa_Batll%C3%B3_01.jpg -->

entries.name: Palau de la Música Catalana
entries.desc: A Catalan Modernisme concert hall by Domènech i Montaner, richly decorated with stained glass, mosaic, and sculpture, including its inverted glass-dome skylight.

photo.alt: Inverted stained-glass skylight dome inside the Palau de la Música Catalana concert hall
photo.credit: <!-- photo: palau-musica-catalana-stained-glass-ceiling.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Palau de la Música Catalana Symphony Hall, Barcelona (Ceiling Tiles and Stained Glass Ceiling)" by Ron Sterling · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Palau_de_la_M%C3%BAsica_Catalana_Symphony_Hall,_Barcelona_(Ceiling_Tiles_and_Stained_Glass_Ceiling).jpg -->

entries.name: Casa Milà (La Pedrera)
entries.desc: Gaudí's stone apartment building nicknamed "the quarry," famed for its rippling limestone facade, wrought-iron balconies, and sculptural rooftop chimneys.

photo.alt: Street-level view of the rippling stone facade of Casa Milà (La Pedrera), Barcelona
photo.credit: <!-- photo: casa-mila-la-pedrera.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "La Pedrera (Casa Mila), Barcelona" by Andrew Bone · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:La_Pedrera_(Casa_Mila),_Barcelona_(26531302331).jpg -->

entries.name: Guggenheim Museum Bilbao
entries.desc: Frank Gehry's titanium-clad museum of curving, sculptural volumes on the Nervión riverfront, credited with reviving the city through cultural tourism.

photo.alt: Titanium-clad curving volumes of the Guggenheim Museum Bilbao on the Nervión riverfront
photo.credit: <!-- photo: guggenheim-museum-bilbao-riverfront.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC0 · "Bilbao - Guggenheim 63" by Zarateman · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Bilbao_-_Guggenheim_63.jpg -->

entries.name: Taipei 101
entries.desc: A Taipei supertall tower once the world's tallest, its tapering stack of tiered sections evoking a segmented bamboo stalk, with a large tuned mass damper inside.

photo.alt: Upward view of Taipei 101's tiered, bamboo-like tapering tower
photo.credit: <!-- photo: taipei-101.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Taipei 101.JPG" by Firelam · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Taipei_101.JPG -->

entries.name: Hagia Sophia
entries.desc: Built under Emperor Justinian, its vast pendentive dome redefined Byzantine architecture; later a mosque and museum, it remains a landmark of Istanbul.

photo.alt: Dome and minaret of Hagia Sophia, Istanbul
photo.credit: <!-- photo: hagia-sophia-facade.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Hagia Sophia (Aya Sofya) front facade, Istanbul, Turkey" by l0da_ralta · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Hagia_Sophia_(Aya_Sofya)_front_facade,_Istanbul,_Turkey_(9606808746).jpg -->

entries.name: Topkapi Palace
entries.desc: The primary administrative center of the Ottoman Empire for nearly 400 years, a sprawling hilltop complex of courtyards, pavilions, and tile-clad chambers overlooking the Bosphorus.

photo.alt: Gate of Salutation entrance towers at Topkapi Palace, Istanbul
photo.credit: <!-- photo: topkapi-palace.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Topkapi Palace (Topkapı Sarayı), Istanbul, Turkey" by l0da_ralta · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Topkapi_Palace_(Topkap%C4%B1_Saray%C4%B1),_Istanbul,_Turkey_(9603571659).jpg -->

meta: Nearly 400 Years as Seat of Power

entries.name: Sheikh Zayed Grand Mosque
entries.tag: Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates · Contemporary Islamic
entries.desc: One of the largest mosques in the world, its gleaming white marble exterior inlaid with semi-precious stones, with a prayer hall housing one of the world's largest hand-knotted carpets and a Swarovski-crystal chandelier.

photo.alt: Main prayer hall of the Sheikh Zayed Grand Mosque with its central chandelier and marble columns
photo.credit: <!-- photo: sheikh-zayed-grand-mosque-prayer-hall.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Abu dhabi, Moschea dello Sceicco Zayed, aula di preghiera, 01" by Francesco Bini (Sailko) · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Abu_dhabi,_Moschea_dello_Sceicco_Zayed,_aula_di_preghiera,_01.jpg -->

entries.name: Burj Khalifa
entries.desc: The tallest building in the world, a tapering, buttressed-core tower in Dubai designed by Adrian Smith of SOM with observation decks near the top.

photo.alt: Burj Khalifa rising above the Dubai skyline
photo.credit: <!-- photo: burj-khalifa.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "Burj Khalifa" by Laika ac · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Burj_Khalifa_(16260269606).jpg -->

entries.name: Louvre Abu Dhabi
entries.tag: Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates · Contemporary
entries.desc: Jean Nouvel's museum on Saadiyat Island, sheltered under a vast latticed dome that filters sunlight into a shifting pattern he called a rain of light.

photo.alt: Sunlight filtering through the latticed dome over the reflecting pool at Louvre Abu Dhabi
photo.credit: <!-- photo: louvre-abu-dhabi.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Louvre abu dhabi, interno, cortile coperto 02" by Francesco Bini · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Louvre_abu_dhabi,_interno,_cortile_coperto_02.jpg -->

entries.name: Museum of the Future
entries.desc: A Dubai museum shaped as a torus, or hollow ring, its stainless-steel facade inscribed with Arabic calligraphy that doubles as the building's windows.

photo.alt: Museum of the Future's illuminated torus-shaped facade at night
photo.credit: <!-- photo: museum-of-the-future-dubai.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Museum of the Future" by Александр Сигачёв · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Museum_of_the_Future.jpg -->

entries.name: Stonehenge
entries.desc: A prehistoric circular arrangement of massive standing stones on Salisbury Plain, built in phases over 1,500 years; its alignment with the summer solstice sunrise suggests astronomical intent, though its true purpose remains debated.

photo.alt: The standing stones of Stonehenge on Salisbury Plain
photo.credit: <!-- photo: stonehenge.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Stonehenge, Condado de Wiltshire, Inglaterra, 2014-08-12, DD 02" by Diego Delso · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Stonehenge,_Condado_de_Wiltshire,_Inglaterra,_2014-08-12,_DD_02.JPG -->

entries.name: Westminster Abbey
entries.desc: A Gothic abbey church that has hosted English and British coronations and royal burials for centuries, adjoining the Palace of Westminster.

photo.alt: The twin west towers and Gothic facade of Westminster Abbey
photo.credit: <!-- photo: westminster-abbey.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Westminster Abbey facade London 2016 (14)" by Paasikivi · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Westminster_Abbey_facade_London_2016_(14).JPG -->

entries.name: 30 St Mary Axe (The Gherkin)
entries.desc: A tapering, glass-clad office tower in the City of London by Norman Foster, whose rounded form and spiraling pattern earned it the nickname the Gherkin.

photo.alt: 30 St Mary Axe (the Gherkin) rising above the City of London
photo.credit: <!-- photo: the-gherkin-london.jpg — Wikimedia Commons Public domain · "30 St Mary Axe, 'Gherkin'" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:30_St_Mary_Axe,_%27Gherkin%27.JPG -->

entries.name: The Shard
entries.desc: Renzo Piano's tapering glass tower beside London Bridge station, among the tallest buildings in the United Kingdom, with a public viewing gallery near the top.

photo.alt: The Shard's tapering glass spire against the London sky
photo.credit: <!-- photo: the-shard-london.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "London - The Shard" by Fred Romero · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:London_-_The_Shard.jpg -->

entries.name: Chrysler Building
entries.tag: New York, United States · Art Deco
entries.desc: A Manhattan skyscraper by William Van Alen, an icon of Art Deco design crowned by its distinctive stainless-steel sunburst spire.

photo.alt: The Chrysler Building's Art Deco spire amid the Manhattan skyline
photo.credit: <!-- photo: chrysler-building.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Chrysler Building from ESB" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Chrysler_Building_from_ESB.JPG -->

entries.name: Empire State Building
entries.tag: New York, United States · Art Deco
entries.desc: An Art Deco skyscraper in Midtown Manhattan that was the world's tallest building for decades, with observation decks near its summit.

photo.alt: New York skyline at dusk with the Empire State Building's illuminated Art Deco spire rising above Midtown
photo.credit: <!-- photo: empire-state-building.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "View of Empire State Building from Rockefeller Center New York City dllu.jpg" by Dllu · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:View_of_Empire_State_Building_from_Rockefeller_Center_New_York_City_dllu.jpg -->

entries.name: Flatiron Building
entries.desc: An early steel-framed Beaux-Arts skyscraper whose narrow triangular footprint, filling a wedge-shaped block, made it an enduring symbol of New York.

photo.alt: The triangular Flatiron Building rising over a Manhattan avenue intersection
photo.credit: <!-- photo: flatiron-building.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "Flatiron Building, Manhattan, New York, USA.jpg" by dconvertini · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Flatiron_Building,_Manhattan,_New_York,_USA.jpg -->

entries.name: Fallingwater
entries.tag: Mill Run, Pennsylvania, United States · Organic Modern
entries.desc: Frank Lloyd Wright's house cantilevered over a waterfall in the Pennsylvania woods, a defining work of his organic architecture.

photo.alt: Frank Lloyd Wright's Fallingwater house cantilevered directly over a wooded Pennsylvania waterfall
photo.credit: <!-- photo: fallingwater.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC0 · "Fallingwater - DSC05639.JPG" by Daderot · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Fallingwater_-_DSC05639.JPG -->

entries.name: Farnsworth House
entries.tag: Plano, Illinois, United States · International Style
entries.desc: Mies van der Rohe's steel-and-glass weekend house raised above a floodplain, a minimalist landmark of the International Style.

photo.alt: Mies van der Rohe's steel-and-glass Farnsworth House raised above its wooded floodplain site
photo.credit: <!-- photo: farnsworth-house.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Farnsworth House by Mies Van Der Rohe - exterior-8.jpg" by Victor Grigas · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Farnsworth_House_by_Mies_Van_Der_Rohe_-_exterior-8.jpg -->

entries.name: Gateway Arch
entries.tag: St. Louis, Missouri, United States · Neo-Futurist
entries.desc: A stainless-steel catenary arch on the St. Louis riverfront by Eero Saarinen, the tallest arch in the world and the tallest man-made monument in the United States, with a tram to an observation room at the apex.

photo.alt: The stainless-steel Gateway Arch rising above the St. Louis riverfront park
photo.credit: <!-- photo: gateway-arch-st-louis.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "Gateway Arch, Gateway Arch National Park, St. Louis, MO - 53052550346.jpg" by w_lemay · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Gateway_Arch,_Gateway_Arch_National_Park,_St._Louis,_MO_-_53052550346.jpg -->

entries.name: TWA Flight Center
entries.desc: Eero Saarinen's winged concrete terminal at JFK Airport, an expressive mid-century landmark now restored as the lobby of a hotel.

photo.alt: Eero Saarinen's winged concrete TWA Flight Center terminal at JFK Airport
photo.credit: <!-- photo: twa-flight-center-jfk.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "TWA Flight Center 2015 NY2.jpg" by Acroterion · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:TWA_Flight_Center_2015_NY2.jpg -->

entries.name: Salk Institute
entries.tag: La Jolla, California, United States · Brutalist
entries.desc: Louis Kahn's research campus of teak and concrete laboratories flanking an open travertine plaza that frames a channel of water toward the Pacific.

photo.alt: Louis Kahn's travertine plaza between the concrete-and-teak laboratory blocks of the Salk Institute
photo.credit: <!-- photo: salk-institute.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "2019 Salk Institute and cafe from west.jpg" by Beyond My Ken · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:2019_Salk_Institute_and_cafe_from_west.jpg -->

entries.name: Seagram Building
entries.tag: New York, United States · International Style
entries.desc: A bronze-and-glass office tower by Mies van der Rohe set back behind a granite plaza, a defining model of the International Style skyscraper.

photo.alt: Mies van der Rohe's bronze-and-glass Seagram Building tower set back on Park Avenue
photo.credit: <!-- photo: seagram-building.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "Seagram Building (35098307116) (cropped).jpg" by Ken OHYAMA · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Seagram_Building_(35098307116)_(cropped).jpg -->

entries.name: Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum
entries.tag: New York, United States · Organic Modern
entries.desc: Frank Lloyd Wright's spiraling white rotunda on Fifth Avenue, where a continuous ramp winds upward past the art displayed along its walls.

photo.alt: The Guggenheim Museum's spiraling white rotunda facade on Fifth Avenue, New York City
photo.credit: <!-- photo: guggenheim-museum-nyc-facade.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum (48059131351).jpg" by Ajay Suresh · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Solomon_R._Guggenheim_Museum_(48059131351).jpg -->

entries.name: Kimbell Art Museum
entries.tag: Fort Worth, Texas, United States · Modernist
entries.desc: Louis Kahn's museum in Fort Worth, a series of parallel cycloid vaults of travertine and concrete whose silver-thin skylights run the full length of the roof and bathe the galleries in diffused natural light — widely considered the finest small art museum ever built.

photo.alt: One of the Kimbell Art Museum's parallel cycloid vaults in travertine and concrete, Fort Worth
photo.credit: <!-- photo: kimbell-art-museum-vaults.jpg — Wikimedia Commons Public Domain · "Kimbell Art Museum Fort Worth 02.jpg" by Andreas Praefcke · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Kimbell_Art_Museum_Fort_Worth_02.jpg -->

entries.name: Walt Disney Concert Hall
entries.desc: Frank Gehry's downtown Los Angeles concert hall, home of the LA Philharmonic, sheathed in billowing stainless-steel panels around a vineyard-style auditorium.

photo.alt: Walt Disney Concert Hall's billowing stainless-steel panels in downtown Los Angeles
photo.credit: <!-- photo: walt-disney-concert-hall.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Walt Disney Concert Hall 2013.jpg" by Tuxyso · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Walt_Disney_Concert_Hall_2013.jpg -->

entries.name: Apple Park
entries.desc: Apple's corporate campus by Foster + Partners, a ring-shaped main building of curved glass set within landscaped grounds in Silicon Valley.

photo.alt: Aerial view of Apple Park's ring-shaped main building set within landscaped grounds, Cupertino
photo.credit: <!-- photo: apple-park-cupertino-aerial.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Aerial view of Apple Park dllu.jpg" by Daniel L. Lu (dllu) · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Aerial_view_of_Apple_Park_dllu.jpg -->

entries.name: One World Trade Center
entries.desc: The tallest building in the United States, built at the World Trade Center site by David Childs of SOM, tapering from a square base to an antenna spire.

photo.alt: One World Trade Center tapering from its square base to its antenna spire against the Manhattan skyline
photo.credit: <!-- photo: one-world-trade-center.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "One World Trade Center, New York (33224081040).jpg" by Pedro Szekely · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:One_World_Trade_Center,_New_York_(33224081040).jpg -->

entries.name: The Vessel
entries.tag: New York, United States · Interactive Sculpture
entries.desc: A honeycomb-like structure of interlocking staircases at Hudson Yards designed by Thomas Heatherwick, built as a climbable centerpiece for the plaza.

photo.alt: The Vessel's honeycomb of interlocking copper-toned staircases at Hudson Yards, New York City
photo.credit: <!-- photo: the-vessel-hudson-yards.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "The Vessel, Hudson Yards, New York City, June 2019.jpg" by Stefan Kemmerling · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:The_Vessel,_Hudson_Yards,_New_York_City,_June_2019.jpg -->

entries.name: St. Peter's Basilica
entries.desc: The principal church of the Vatican, shaped by Renaissance and Baroque masters, with Michelangelo's dome and Bernini's colonnaded square before it.

photo.alt: St. Peter's Basilica's facade and Michelangelo's dome rising over Vatican City
photo.credit: <!-- photo: st-peters-basilica-facade.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC0 · "Saint Peter's Basilica facade, Rome, Italy.jpg" by Jebulon · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Saint_Peter's_Basilica_facade,_Rome,_Italy.jpg -->
